sentences.text: Completely gone.
And my mother didn't know where to go or what to do.
Two years.
Well, when we got out of it, that's the last I had ever seen of it.
I don't believe so because I know they took everything out of it that anybody wanted.
And-- and then the bombing started and it was destroyed, so.
I don't know, maybe it was the Americans.
I have no idea.
Maybe the Russians.
Must have been the Russians because they were our liberators, probably.
My cousin's house was also destroyed.
The one that was right next to us, that was also destroyed.
Dismay.
They couldn't believe that this tiny little place was like gone, very few things survived.
And everybody was just like walking in a daze.
Yes, whoever came up with us.
You know, we were all there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of them didn't live exactly where I did.
Some of them lived a few other places.
But at that point, my mother, with me, she didn't know where to go, where to turn, what to do, where to stay.
So she went over to an officer, a Russian officer.
And she said to him, I'm a very good cook, I will cook for you if you give me lodging, a place to stay with my child.
So he said, OK.
And he took her to a place where a lot of the Russians were and gave us a little corner.
And he said that we could stay there.
And in turn, she did exactly what she said-- she cooked.
And I told you, she was so wonderful cook.
And they were so thrilled with her cooking, they loved it so much, they wanted to do something for her.
So she says, the first thing she says, I want you to help me find my niece-- I mean, get my niece, my cousin who lived next door to me.
She was the only one from six children who worked for this farm lady as a hired hand.
But because she was blond, had blue eyes, and didn't have that so-called Jewish look, she was able to keep her on the farm.
And she stayed there the whole two years.
While we were hiding, we knew where she was.
But being afraid not to give her away, we couldn't go near her or talk to her, didn't want to get her life in danger.
But my mother swore to herself that some day, if she ever makes this through, she is going to get her away from her.
And that's what's the first thing.
The officer said OK.
And my mother went in a jeep with this officer and they drove up to this farmhouse and they got my cousin.
That was Malla.
She now lives in Israel.
Malla at that time must have been about 13 or 14 years old, or maybe 10 years.
I'm not certain of the time.
Somewheres in there.
And my mother has said, she is my flesh and blood, she is coming with me, she cannot stay here anymore.
And the lady was very upset and she started to cry.
And she said, I sacrificed my life to allow her to be here.
How can you take her away now.
And my mother said, I have to take her away.
I thank you with all my heart for saving her life.
But she belongs with us.
And they took her.
And then my mother-- she stayed with us.
And she more or less babysat [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for me while my mother continued working.
And I guess a few weeks, a few months went by.
And these officers were just thrilled with my mother's cooking.
And they said, we have a big surprise for you.
Well, she didn't know what it was.
They said, you come with us.
And she went with them and they took her to this place in a basement.
She walked down and there was a German officer sitting on a chair with his hands tied back.
And they said to her, here is a gun, you can shoot him because he is one of the people that killed your people.
My mother took the gun in her hand and she looked at him.
And she said, I can't do that, I cannot do that, I cannot take a life like that.
Even though they did such terrible things, I can't do that.
And she turned around and cried and walked out.
And as she went up stairs, she heard the gun go off.
They killed him for her.
She says, I couldn't do that.
That was another instant.
Just being-- going through the rubble, you know, and making up childhood games.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never had any toys.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You just make do.
It's amazing what a human body can withstand and what a person can really-- the imagination, I had to work a lot with imagination.
And that's how we worked it.
The Russians.
And they kept us fed, we started to eat.
And they gave us a place to stay.
In the interim, more and more people started coming in.
And our former neighbor who used to be across the street from us, she and her five children were very fortunate because one farmer lady kept them the whole time.
So she was alive with all her children and her husband, they all survived.
[SIGHS] And people used to come to town and they would look and they would find each other.
You know, it was such a miracle to think that somebody was alive.
And of course, they knew my mother very well because they knew my mother, they knew my father, they knew all the children.
We lived across the street.
And like I said, it was a very small place.
One thing led to another and they started talking and she said, my brother also survived.
And he lost his whole family.
And the two of you are alone.
I think maybe you should get married.
And that was David Bichman.
My mother didn't want to, but she wanted to get some kind of a home for me and my cousin.
So she thought maybe if she married him-- he used to have a farm place-- maybe we'd have a home.
And she was able to get us away from that little corner we were in.
So she married him.
No, not at all, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I still I think was thinking about my father.
And I think I was a little resentful.
We continued living as a family.
And my cousin was with us at the time.
And he-- as a few years went by, my cousin met somebody that was in the service.
And they made a match and they got married.
In the Russian service.
He had lost a leg in the war, but he was a very nice, dear person.
And my cousin and he, they got together and they got married.
So my mother made them-- well, I don't know what kind of a wedding it was, but they got together, anyways.
And then it was my mother and I and my stepfather.
And the whole hope was to-- we have to push on, we have to get away from here, we have to get to Palestine.
That was the whole drive, is to get to Israel.
Maybe '44.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm not certain of the year. '45, something like that.
Yes, absolutely.
This is how they all-- one would tell the other.
This is what we must do.
You must make plans, you must get out there, you have to fill out forms.
And it was like, fill out forms, and then we did.
And then they put us on a cattle car.
I remember, we finally got some place and we were driving and driving and going and going.
Yes.
As far as I know, they did.
I don't think they wanted us there.
I mean, they were happy to see if we could get into Israel.
The process sort of went on.
No, not at that time.
Well, we didn't have anything, so I don't know how they did it.
But they made our papers.
And we got to-- next thing I knew, we ended up in Germany.
It was a lot of trains, stand up trains, you know where, with no seats, where everybody sat on the floor.
It was like cars.
Yes.
It was difficult because it was hot.
It was very little food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was a child.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that made it that much more difficult for my mother to cope with.
It seemed to be very crowded.
It seemed--
We were all like sitting around.
There was a whole bunch of people.
Oh, absolutely.
They were all survivors like ourselves.
Israel.
That was the key thing was to get to Israel.
In order to get there, we had to get to the German side.
And that's where they had DP camps.
Displaced Person campus.
They had to get out of there.
They lost too much.
The Jewish land.
Hopefully, the land of freedom where nobody will kill you, that nobody will call you you dirty Jew.
It was for a new start, a new beginning.
And that was the hope of all the people going there.
Next year, Yerushalayim.
That was the big hope and the big dream.
Whenever you prayed, you always prayed, next year, Yerushalayim.
We should be in Israel.
And we got to these DP camps.
And that was run by the Americans.
That was already like going into heaven because they had barracks for us, long army barracks.
And they gave us food and they gave us drink.
And each family was assigned a little room of our own.
And when they gave us a cot, we could sleep on a cot.
And green army blankets.
I remember those green army blankets.
Americans.
Americans were running that camp.
And that was-- there is this wonderful luck.
We can walk in the daytime and not hide.
And that's when I thought, this is so great.
And being very clever, you had to make things out of whatever you had.
I had no clothing.
And my mother took a green army blanket and she made me like a snowsuit out of it, you know, pants and a top.
Probably like that.
We went from [GERMAN], we went to [?
Ulm. ?] And the best thing I remember was Pocking.
Pocking in Germany was the one place that I remember because life seemed so good, from a kid's point-of-view.
They were getting us closer and closer.
They had quotas on how many can go to Israel.
It was set up so that the orphans go first.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, and, and they had established like a kibbutz type of atmosphere for a lot of the children who had no parents.
And in order to get on it, my mother put me into it.
And she hoped that, by me being in it-- and she again would be like a worker helping with the children, and also my stepfather-- that we'd be able to get to Israel faster.
That was our only chance of really getting there.
And it worked.
We were in this kibbutz.
And but my mother, in back of her mind, always knew, she always, always knew that she had brothers and sisters in America.
She was the youngest of 13 children.
So she went to the newspaper place, Munich, Germany.
And she placed an ad for the American papers.
And she described all her brothers and sisters that she could remember.
And she gave them where she had-- was living at the time.
And she said, please, if anybody is out there, get in touch with me and let me know.
Well, time went on, we didn't hear anything.
By now, we got our notice that we're getting ready to go to Israel.
So our packs, everybody started packing whatever little belongings we had.
And we marked, you know, Israel on all our whatever we had.
When my mother received a telegram, and it seems like a cousin in Evansville, Indiana was reading the newspaper.
And she comes across all these names that she knows.
And she immediately started making phone calls.
My mother had a brother in New Jersey, a sister in California, and another brother here in Maine.
And the phone calls were just going from one to another.
It was just like a relay thing, you know.
And things started working immediately and they started saying, we have to get her out of there.
And we received a telegram saying, we have your name, we're getting papers, don't do anything, don't go anywhere.
We're going to take you to America.
And that was like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an unbelievable thing that could have possibly happened, but it happened.
And this is when we started-- she took me out of there and she said, my child will not be going.
We're going to America.
The golden country, land of opportunity.
They made out papers for us.

And they had to sign with the American government that they will not be-- the government will not be responsible for us, that our family will take on all responsibility as far as housing, clothing, a place to live, and they'll get a job for us.
They will take on everything just so they can come out.
No, no, we would have been Israelis.
I would have been talking Hebrew to you now.
No, we would have been Israelis, absolutely.
My mother, my mother always knew.
She always when she had to make another try.
She just had to try.
No, she never had the opportunity before.
But she wanted to try and see, not knowing where they were or what cities they were at.
She just placed an ad and hoped that somebody, somewhere would see it.
And that's exactly what happened.
Cattle boat.
I know, it was cattle boat.
It was the most horrendous trip ever.
The eating places were like, instead of flat where you put, It was like this.
But it didn't matter.
It took 11 days.
Yes, a lot of people were on it.
Absolutely, all survivors.
[SIGHS] A lot of joy, a lot of enthusiasm to look forward to the, the golden Medina, you know, the golden country.
You're going to America.
The most wonderful thing in the world.
And they told me, you, you're going to go to America.
I mean, this is a chance of a lifetime.
You're going to America.
So being sick the whole nine days before we landed didn't matter, because we were going to America.
And the most wonderful thing was coming into the harbor and seeing the Statue of Liberty.
I didn't know about the Statue of Liberty.
But her standing out there gave you the most wonderful feeling.
It's like we're coming home.
For some reason, it was a wonderful feeling.
How old were you at the time?
I came to America when I was 12 years old.
October 19, 1949.
It will be always with me.
I'll never forget that date-- the beginning of a life.
We came off the boat, and I think that we were in New York.
And I think they get notices of boats arriving, so they have a lot of Jewish people that come to the boats to see if there are any relatives or anybody that they might know that's there.
And they would check things over.
And they would come, and they of course all spoke Yiddish.
And they talked to us.
And they said to my mother and stepfather, where, where is your destination.
And just as proud as can be, I stood up and I said, Portland, Maine.
You know like, this is it.
And the guy looked at me and he said, Portland, Maine.
You're going to schlep this kid all the way to Portland, Maine?
And I was so insulted to think that, what does he mean saying such a thing.
Oh, he says, you don't want to go to Portland, Maine.
But this is our home, this is where we're going.
And this is how it was.
My uncle, my cousins, they all greeted us at the station
Portland.
No, we had-- we went from the station in New York by train to Maine.
The trip was very exciting.
One more train trip.
But this was a happy train trip, a very happy one.
And I couldn't wait, I couldn't wait.
I don't know how long it took, but it wasn't long, in my opinion.
I couldn't wait to get here.
And my mother seeing her brother for the first time was quite thrilling.
And, and then after a while, they got the brother from New Jersey.
And it was like a family reunion.
She had somebody else that was a part of her family.
Yiddish, we spoke Yiddish, of course.
Yes, but they didn't speak Yiddish and I didn't speak English.
I was 12 years old.
That was another problem.
I was 12 years old and I didn't know how to read, write, or speak English.
And they wanted-- they had to start me in school.
Where do you put a 12-year-old who doesn't speak it, read it, write it, or anything?
You know, I was like a dummy.
So they took me into grade school.
I think it was the sixth grade.
And the teacher who was wonderful-- Mrs.
Ferguson.
I had a Mrs.
Ferguson and a Mrs.
Murphy, whom I'll never forget.
They took to me and they took the brightest child out of each class and sit with me in the back.
And they would bring up little books with the A, B, you know, the alphabet.
And little by little, and it took a long time before I said anything because I was so afraid I would say it wrong.
Now coming to this country, I spoke fluent Russian and Polish.
And I understood German, of course.
And Yiddish was my native tongue.
But coming here, I was so tired of being the odd person, of everything being wrong.
I wanted to fit in.
I didn't want to speak Russian or anything else.
I pushed it away from me.
I just wanted to concentrate on English.
My mother spoke long before me because she spoke broken English, but that was OK.
But being a child, if you said something that wasn't correct, they would laugh at you.
I couldn't take having them laugh at me.
So I didn't speak.
And the teacher was wonderful.
She tried to get me to speak.
I couldn't say anything, I couldn't get it out until I knew in my mind that what I'm saying is right.
We were coming from recess, going up the stairs, and one of the kids accidentally caught the back of the heel of my shoe.
And I turned quickly and said, excuse me.
That was the first American word I said.
